coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as fluorescent platforms for recognition of fluoride ion via the inner filter effect of simple arylboronic acids. Experimental and theoretical investigations
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadpour, Zahra
Safavi, Afsaneh
Omidvar, Akbar
Mohajeri, Afshan
Mobaraki, Nabiollah
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new type of water soluble fluorescent assay was designed for fluoride ion based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of simple para-substituted arylboronic acids. Carbon nanodots (CDs) as the green and nontoxic carnbonic nanomaterials were used as the fluorophores and arylboronic acids as the absorbers. The reaction of fluoride ion with boron center of arylboronic acids tuned the absorption profiles toward longer or shorter wavelengths. In case of an increase in the spectral overlap between absorber and fluorophore, a decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed and considered as a response for quantitative fluoride measurement. The minimum detectable value of fluoride by these assays fulfilled the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) requirement for water safety. The simple design of this assay together with avoidance of any covalent linkage between fluorophore and absorbers offers a cost effective, selective and sensitive fluoride sensor in aqueous environments. Furthermore, we have carried out density functional theory calculations to study the electronic structures and optical absorption spectra of the arylboronic acids before and after reaction with fluoride. Excellent agreement between the experimental result and theoretical calculations enables for prediction of spectral change of aryl boronic acids upon reaction with fluoride. In particular, the effect of fluoridation reaction on the excitation spectrum of CDs through IFE could be simply predicted by the theoretical calculation. Therefore, by using the present system, design of even more successful probes would be feasible for fluoride recognition in aqueous media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arylboronic acids
Fluoride nanosensor
Inner filter effect
Twisted intramolecular charge transfer
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Molecular structures and the quenching mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The support of this work by Iran National Elite Foundation and Shiraz University Research Council is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Lewis acid-base and (B) BrØnsted acid-base reaction of arylboronic acids with fluoride acting as nucleophile and base, respectively.
Schematic illustration of LE (locally excited) and TICT (twisted intramolecular charge transfer) states ofDMAPB. The chemical structure of DMAPB is also shown.
(A) TEM image, (B) absorption(a), excitation (b) and emission spectra (c), (C) FTIR spectrum of CDs.

Overlapping of the UV spectra of arylboronic acid and the corresponding fluorinated products with the excitation wavelength of CDs. Transformation of arylboronic acids to trifluoroarylboronates is represented in panels (A) for MPBA, (C) MTPBA, (D) PBA and (E) NPBA. (B) is the proton exchange reaction of MPBA and transformation into thiolate ligand.

The UV spectral change of MPBA (curve a) upon deprotonationof SH group (curve b) and fluoridationon the boronic acid head (curve c) by fluoride. (Inset: MPBA absorption change promoted by the addition of OH−).

Fluorescence spectrum of CDs/MPBA mixture with incremental addition of fluoride, measured in KCl (0.1M), λex 300nm. (Inset: Titration curves against fluoride in KCl (0.1M) (circle) and 0.05M phosphate-acetate buffer, pH 4 (triangle)).

(A) Linear plot of fluorescence intensity ratios of CDs/MPBA mixture versus fluoride concentrations. (B). Titration curves against fluoride, chloride, bromide and dihydrogen phosphate. ([MPBA]=2mM in KCl (0.1M) with starting pH point of 4, λex 300nm).

(A) Comparison of the UV response of NPBA and PBA upon fluoride addition, (B) Linear plot of fluorescence intensity of CDs/NPBA mixture versus fluoride([NPBA]=0.5mM in phosphate-acetate buffer 0.05M, pH 4, λex 300nm) (C) Fluorescence response of fluoride compared to those of other anions.

(A) Excitation and emission spectra of DMAPB, (B) Fluoride effect on the synchronous emission spectrum of DMAPB, measured at constant pH of 4, Δλ 180nm, (C) selectivity of the DMAPB response towards fluoride ion with respect to other anions.

Spatial distributions of frontier molecular orbitals of the boronic acid and fluorinated absorbers. The red and green colors stand for electrons and holes, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Gibbs free energy diagram for the fluoridation of boronic acid moieties present in Scheme 1.
The Hammet constant of substituted arylboronic acids studied in this work and corresponding binding constant of fluoridation reaction in aqueous media.
Theoretical (experimental) wavelength of the dominant peak in UV–visible spectra. Δλ shows the shift in wavelength after fluorination (all values are in nm).

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as fluorescent platforms for recognition of fluoride ion via the inner filter effect of simple arylboronic acids. Experimental and theoretical investigations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 7194684795, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy designed for fluoride sensing in aqueous media through the inner filter effect of arylboronic acids on fluorescent carbon nanodots.

In this work, a new type of water soluble fluorescent assay was designed for fluoride ion based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of simple para-substituted arylboronic acids. Carbon nanodots (CDs) as the green and nontoxic carnbonic nanomaterials were used as the fluorophores and arylboronic acids as the absorbers. The reaction of fluoride ion with boron center of arylboronic acids tuned the absorption profiles toward longer or shorter wavelengths. In case of an increase in the spectral overlap between absorber and fluorophore, a decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed and considered as a response for quantitative fluoride measurement. The minimum detectable value of fluoride by these assays fulfilled the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) requirement for water safety. The simple design of this assay together with avoidance of any covalent linkage between fluorophore and absorbers offers a cost effective, selective and sensitive fluoride sensor in aqueous environments. Furthermore, we have carried out density functional theory calculations to study the electronic structures and optical absorption spectra of the arylboronic acids before and after reaction with fluoride. Excellent agreement between the experimental result and theoretical calculations enables for prediction of spectral change of aryl boronic acids upon reaction with fluoride. In particular, the effect of fluoridation reaction on the excitation spectrum of CDs through IFE could be simply predicted by the theoretical calculation. Therefore, by using the present system, design of even more successful probes would be feasible for fluoride recognition in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:para: The importance of fluoride detection associated with various environmental and health-care issues is well recognized all around the world. Strongly dependent on the exposure value of this ion, it can be either a drug or simply a killer. From the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) standpoint [1], above 4ppm of fluoride in drinking water has been considered a serious health risk.

Molecular receptors are widely used materials for anion recognition [2–7]. However, most of fluoride receptors with huge organic backbone are not soluble in water. There has been many reports of using these molecules in biphasic conditions [8] or water/organic solvent (DMSO, MeOH, …) mixtures [9,10]. In contrary to organic receptors, nanosensors designed so far have successfully transferred anion recognition platforms from organic solvents to water. Some examples of nanoparticle based probes include use of gold nanoparticles [11] and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [12,13] for fluoride sensing. Carbon nanodots (CDs) as the biocompatible version of toxic QDs which have been extensively reviewed recently, are suitable candidates in sensing ionic or molecular targets [14–16]. High luminescence quantum yield, stability, ease of synthesis and high water solubility make these carbon nanomaterials appealing to researchers [17,18]. They have been used to develop sensitive assays for anions such as C2O4 2− [19], PO4 3− [20], CN− [21], F− [22,23], S2− [24], ClO− [25], and I− [26]. These sensors are mostly based on the restoration of fluorescence signal previously quenched by metal cations. For example, an “on-off-on” strategy has been reported based on the recovery of CDs fluorescence signal quenched by metal cations [22]. Although this probe operate successfully in water but the signal recovery process triggers from high concentrations of fluoride, much higher than the maximum permitted value by EPA. Another report is a fluorescent probe based on CDs functionalized with carboxyl groups on the surface for fluoride sensing. Coordination of Zr(H2O)2EDTA to the surface of CDs enhance the luminescence while addition of fluoride turn the signal to the “off” state. The mechanism has been illustrated by competitive ligand reactions between fluoride and surface carboxyl groups to Zr complex [27].

Herein, small organic boron based compounds originated from arylboronic acids have been used for fluoride capturing in aqueous media. A combined experimental and computational study has been reported on these molecules, aimed to introduce simple sensing systems for fluoride ion recognition in aqueous solution. There has been extensive reports on boron based Lewis acids as one of the most popular receptors in this area [28]. For an efficient fluoride capturing in aqueous environment, the Lewis acidity power should be modulated to dominate the high hydration enthalpy of fluoride as a small anion. In fact, introduction of electronegative oxygen substituent to arylboranes [29] as well as coordination of transition metals to the π system [30] appear well suited for the complexation of fluoride. Other alternative approach for enhancing the acidity power is using cationic boranes [31]. The cationic functionality adjacent to the boron atom will attract oppositely charged fluoride. Therefore, a stronger interaction with fluoride ions is established compared to the neutral boronic acids [32]. Although the binding constants for these receptors are excellent in organic solvents, some of them are even not stable in aqueous solution [33].

In this study, electron withdrawing groups like nitro as well as cationic substituents such as protonated amine has incorporated on the phenyl ring to enhance the Lewis acidity of the receptors. This could directly affect the fluoride affinity as a nucleophile. Meanwhile, fluoride can react as a basic anion and take part in proton exchange reaction with BrØnsted acidic substituents. The sensing strategy in this work is simply illustrated via the inner filter effect (IFE) of CDs and arylboronic acids as the absorbers. While IFE has been considered as a problem in fluorescence measurement and should be avoided [34,35], there are some reports of using this effect in developing new fluorescent assays with no need of covalent linking between the two parties [36–38]. There are only a few CD-based fluorescent nanosensors via IFE in the literature [39–42] and to the best of our knowledge this is the first attempt of fluoride sensing by CDs via IFE. Here, CDs act as the fluorophore and fluorinated arylboronic acids as simple light absorbers. The reaction of fluoride ion either as a base or nuceophile with simple para-substituted arylboronic acids could shift the absorption spectra of these compounds toward longer or shorter wavelengths. In case of a red shift in the absorption spectra, the excitation light of CDs could be harvested by the absorber and the fluorescence signal decreased. On the other hand, the blue shift had no effect on the fluorescence spectra of CDs. On the theoretical part, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to investigate the spectral shift of these receptors upon reaction with fluoride. Interestingly, the blue or red shift in the position of receptors’ absorption bands observed experimentally were consistent to what predicted by the DFT spectral calculations.

All phenylboronic acids used in this work were purchased commercially without further purification process or treatment. Phenylboronic acid (PBA) and 4-Nitrophenylboronic acid (NPBA) were prepared from Acros. 4-Mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA), 4-methylthiophenylboronic acid (MTPBA) and 4-dimethylaminophenylboronic acid (DMAPB) were prepared from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium fluoride was purchased from Fluka. Mercaptosuccinic acid, sulphuric acid, dichloromethane, ammonia, ethanol and the sodium salt of all other anions were prepared from Merck. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without treatment.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS45) with a 1.0cm quartz cell. The absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1-cm path length quartz cuvette. TEM image was taken by a Zeiss-EM10C device at 80KV. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) spectra were recorded by KBr method using a Shimadzu FTIR 8000 series. pH measurements were carried out using a Metrohm pH meter (Model 780) with a combined pH glass electrode calibrated against standard buffer solutions at pH 4.0, 7.0 and 9.0.

CDs were synthesized from mercaptosuccinic acid as the precursor. According to a reported synthesis protocol [43], 5mL of concentrated sulphuric acid was added to 150mg of precursor and refluxed for 4h. The reaction mixture was then cooled to room temperature and poured into 20mL of water in an ice bath. To remove high concentrated acid, dichloromethane was used to extract pre-CDs from sulphuric acid. The mixture of aqueous and organic solvent was shaken in a separating funnel. This step was repeated several times to ensure that CDs has been transferred into organic phase as much as possible. Dichloromethane was then evaporated and excess ammonium hydroxide was added to dry pre-CDs. By this time Pre-CDs was converted to CDs by polymerization. After 48h, water and ammonia were evaporated at 60°C. The final product was a pale yellow solid.

Stock solutions of all arylboronic acids (5mM) were made in ethanol. All measurements were carried out in acidic pH value (pH 4) which is sufficiently lower than the pka of boronic acids otherwise hydroxide ion compete with fluoridation reactions. Each arylboronic acid (40μM, except for PBA which was 1mM) was prepared either in 10mL of KCl (0.1M) or phosphate-acetate buffer solution(0.05M). Experiments were fluoride considered as a nucleophile were conducted in fixed pH value of 4 while those with fluoride as a basic ion were carried out in KCl (0.1M) with no pH adjustment. In each mode, after fluoride injection to the aqueous solution of arylboronic acid, the UV spectral change was recorded.

To a solution of CDs (1.5μgmL−1), absorbers were added to the final optimum concentration. The optimum concentration used for arylboronic acids were 2mM (MPBA and MTPBA) and 0.5mM (NPBA). The pH was adjusted to the optimum value. Different aliquots of fluoride ion were then injected to this mixture. For each point, after 5min incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. In fixed pH mode, experiments were carried out in phosphate-acetate buffer (0.05M, pH 4) while in variable pH, the experiments were conducted in KCl (0.1M).

DFT computations on small model systems − absorber compounds − have been carried out to rationalize the influence of fluoride substituent (Scheme 1 ) on the optical properties of absorbers and its quenching effect on the CD’s fluorescence. Geometry optimizations as well as frontier molecular orbital (FMO) analyses have been performed with the B3LYP method. All calculations were carried out by using the 6–31G(d) basis set with the help of GAUSSIAN09 suite of programs [44]. All structures of absorber compounds were fully optimized in water media with default convergence criteria. The solute-solvent interaction was modeled using the self-consistent reaction field (SCRF) [45] method. Moreover, frequency calculations were performed to ensure that there are no imaginary frequencies for minima. Zero point energies and thermodynamic functions were calculated at 298.15K and 1atm.

On the basis of time dependent DFT (TDDFT) formalism, the UV–vis absorption spectra were calculated by three density functionals; PB86 [46], B3LYP [47] and CAM-B3LYP [48] belonging to different categories of DFT methods. The details of TDDFT calculations including major molecular orbital contribution, oscillator strengths and excitation wavelengths for all three tested methods were compared in the supporting information. It is observed that PB86 functional remarkably outperforms other tested functionals and its results for wavelength shifts are in better agreement with the experimental values. This outcome is well supported by previous investigations where the PB86 functional have been suggested as efficient method for excited states calculations [49]. Accordingly, all reported TDDFT results are based on PB86 method.

TEM image of small spherical CDs in Fig. 1 A depicts the average size of 5nm. The maximum absorption and excitation wavelength of CDs was pretty much the same at 300nm and the emission wavelength was observed at 405nm (Fig. 1B). The surface functionalities confirmed by FTIR spectrum. Peaks appeared at 3124, 1690, 1317, 3500 and 860cm−1signifies the presence of CH, COOH, CO, OH and CS functional groups, respectively (Fig. 1C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular structures and the quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, arylboronic acids of general formula [X-phenyl-B(OH)2] with X=SH, SMe, NO2, H, NH(Me)2 + were investigated. The molecular structures of para-substituted arylboronic acids and the formation of trifluoroarylboronates are shown in Scheme 1. Lewis acid-base property of boron and fluoride has been well adapted for detection of fluoride ion in apolar media since 1980s [50]. The exceptional strength of B-F bond favors the sequential formation of [PhB(OH)3-xFx]−(for x=1,2,3) in exposure of arylboronic acid to fluoride ion in water [51–53]. While fluoride as a nucleophile drives the formation of powerful B-F bonds, it could also take part in proton exchange reaction of acidic substituent in variable pH mode as in two cases for SH and NH(Me)2 +.

The substituent effect on the Lewis acidity of organic compounds is simply illustrated with the aid of Hammet constant (σ) [54]. A systematic examination of the relationship between pka and σ of arylboronic acids [55] showed that more positive value of σ is equivalent to higher Lewis acidity. The values of σ for these substituents on the para position have been tabulated in Table 1 . Although we did not find the exact value of σ for NH(Me)2 +, it should be somehow between the values for NH3 + and N(Me)3 + [54]. In order to have a general overview of fluoride affinity of these molecules, titration experiments were carried out using UV–vis spectroscopy. The fluoride affinity of these receptors was extracted by calculation of binding constants. The values of binding constants were calculated by fitting the experimental data into series of equations (please see the supporting information). Fitting the experimental titration curves to a 1:3 binding isotherm thereby indicating that the Lewis acidity of receptors increased in the following order: NH(Me)2 + >NO2>SH ≈SMe ≈H (Table 1).Fitting the experimental titration curves afforded larger values of binding constant for electron withdrawing substituents. The trends are in perfect agreement with the acidity scale established by σ. The binding constants of NO2 and NH(Me)2 +substituents are two orders of magnitude larger than the other derivatives. This observation is in support of the higher Lewis acidity of these compounds. It should be noted that the fluoride affinity of electron donor functional groups (S−andN(Me)2) with negative σ values, was quite low. Thus, we did not calculate the binding constant for those substituents. As an example, the spectral change of PBA along with the experimental and fitted data curves has been shown in Fig. S1.

In an effort to have an insight into how this fluorescent assay works for fluoride recognition based on IFE, the spectral overlap between the absorption peak of absorber (before and after reaction with fluoride) and excitation wavelength of CDs has been compared in Fig. 2 . In two cases b and e, reaction of fluoride with arylboronic acid shifts the absorption toward longer wavelengths. As a result the spectral overlap between fluorophore and absorber increases. On the other hand, the blue shift in absorption spectra after reaction with fluoride decreased signal overlapping. Therefore, as will be discussed later, these absorbers have no effect on fluorescence signal of CDs.

According to the reports in the literature [56,57], when the total UV−Vis intensity of the light absorber at the fluorescence excitation and/or emission wavelengths of fluorophore is less than 0.05, no IFE would be observed. In the present study, the concentrations of arylboronic acids in fluorescence measurements were high (0.5mM for NPBA and 2mM for MPBA) and the absorbance values exceeded 0.05. This means that the light absorption in fluorescence measurement through IFE would be inevitable and strong. Upon reaction with fluoride through either fluoridation reaction or deprotonation, the absorption maximum of each of these absorbers shifts to longer wavelength. Consequently, the extent of overlapping with excitation wavelength of CDs was increased and the fluorescence signal decreased accordingly. Conventional photophysical processes including photoinduced electron transfer (PET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), metal− ligand charge transfer (MLCT), electronic energy transfer (EET), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) require the intermolecular interaction between the chemosensor and target molecule [39]. They only work when target and chemosensor are at a particular distance or have a complementary geometry. However, IFE takes place in solution regardless of whether or not the fluorophore is bind to the absorber. In the present work, no binding agent or linker was used to incorporate absorbers on the surface of CDs.

Also, in a report by Yuan et al. [58], a mathematical calculation accounting for different mechanisms of fluorescence modulation by absorbing species (including IFE and resonance energy transfer) was carried out. It was found that the energy transfer process is negligible except at quencher concentrations greater than 4mM. Since the initial concentrations of NPBA and MPBA are 0.5 and 2mM (below 4mM) respectively, the possibility of energy transfer as a quenching pathway is ruled out. Therefore, the respected mechanism is demonstrated to be IFE.

MPBA representing two reaction centers for fluoride ion, one through the fluoridation of boronic acid moiety and the other, proton exchange of SH group. In fact, for this molecule there are two different reaction pathways by taking fluoride either as a basic or a nucleophilic anion. However, as will be discussed in the following paragraphs, deprotonation of SH group favors over the fluoridation reaction and would be considered the reaction pathway which affects the fluorescence signal through the IFE mechanism.

Two sets of experiments in aqueous media, one in buffered solution and the other with variable pH revealed that basic and nucleophilic fluoride affect MPBA in completely two different reaction pathways. Curves (a, b) in Fig. 3 , depict the UV absorbance change of MPBA with the addition of fluoride playing the role of a basic anion. The UV spectrum of 40μM MPBA in KCl (0.1M) and the initial pH value of 4 shows one absorption peak at 255. At this pH which is lower than the pka of SH group (5.67), MPBA is almost fully protonated and this peak could be assigned to the protonated form of MPBA. After the addition of 153mM fluoride ion, the solution pH changed almost 2 units and increased to 6. The peak intensity at 255nm decreased accompanied by a few nanometers red shift and another absorption peak at 287nm developed possibly assign to unprotonated (or thiolate) form of MPBA. Therefore, at higher pH values, SH group deprotonates and both thiol-thiolate forms coexist in solution.

The same experiment by OH− showed a similar trend in spectral change (Inset of Fig. 3). This confirms that by increasing the solution pH, the thiol-thiolate equilibrium gradually changes toward unprotonated species. In this case, fluoride reacts as a basic anion and deprotonates SH group. On the other hand, nucleophilic fluoride was expected to take part in Lewis acid-base reaction through the boronic acid head of MPBA. Fluoridation of the Lewis acidic part is favored in acidic pH values and converts MPBA to trifluoroarylboronate anion. To elucidate this, pH was fixed at 4, therefore only the unprotonated (thiol) form participated in fluoridation reaction. Upon titration with fluoride in fixed pH, a distinct blue shift of the maximum absorbance peak at 255–238nm was observed (curves a, c). As presented earlier in Fig. 2, only the deprotonated MPBA could shield the excitation light of CDs.

The fluorescence change of CDs/MPBA by fluoride in variable pH mode was explored. The effects of some experimental parameters were studied to maximize the fluorescence response. The optimum conditions were 2mM MPBA and initial pH point set to 4 (Fig. S2). The fluoride sensitivity of this molecule was totally affected by MPBA concentration. The calibration curves constructed with different concentrations of MPBA clearly confirmed that the maximum value of the slope was observed with MPBA adjusted to 2mM. Therefore, this concentration was selected as the optimum. With a starting pH point of 4 and 2mM MPBA in 0.1M KCl solution, fluoride gradually decreased the fluorescence signal accompanied by an increase in pH (Fig. 4 ). The correlation of the fluorescence signal change against fluoride concentration suggests a linear range of 0–15mM and a LOD value of 1.46×10−4(Fig. 5 A). This LOD value is lower than the maximum fluoride level permitted by EPA (2.0×10−4 M), thereby demonstrating that MPBA is competent for fluoride sensing in the parts per million range in water. Anions with weak basic properties do not affect the fluorescence signal of CDs/MPBA mixture which is quite encouraging. It is well supported in Fig. 5B that, chloride, bromide and dihydrogen phosphate have negligible effects even at high concentrations. However, basic ions affect the BrØnsted acid-base reaction of this probe. This limitation can be overcome by using the nucleophilic property of fluoride as will be discussed in the following section.

As noted in the previous paragraph, by changing pH toward basic condition, a thiol-thiolate equilibrium establishes and the thiolate form prevails at higher pH values. Thus, by a gradual red shift in the absorption spectrum of MPBA, there was less excitation light available to excite the fluorophore, and the emission intensity decreased correspondingly. In the same concentrations of all species, a similar fluorescence experiment was carried out in 0.05M phosphate-acetate buffer solution with a constant pH of 4. (Inset, Fig. 4). In the fixed pH value of 4, almost 98% of MPBA exists in thiol form. Surprisingly, the fluorescence signal of CDs/MPBA mixture almost did not change by incremental fluoride addition. This also supports the idea that only deprotonated MPBA contributes to IFE. In both types of experiments mentioned above, fluoride was titrated in the range of 0–15mM.

MTPBA as a control molecule was used to further confirm the role of thiolate ligand on signal suppression. The change in fluorescence signal of MTPBA/CDs mixture was monitored upon incremental addition of fluoride in KCl solution. Addition of MTPBA hardly changed the fluorescence signal of CDs. The system response toward fluoride ion in variable pH mode for MPBA and MTPBA is compared in Fig. S3. Both molecules were evaluated under the same experimental conditions. The results clearly show that upon fluoride titration, the contribution of MTPBA to signal suppression was much less than that of MPBA. The deprotonationof SH group of MPBA by fluoride as a basic ion converts this group to thiolate while for MTPBA, the sulphur atom has been blocked by a methyl group. The detachment of methyl group is prevented due to the mild experimental conditions. Therefore, in contrast to MPBA, pH variation does not affect MTPBA. On the other hand, as explained earlier, the absorbance blue shift of MTPBA in buffered condition prevents IFE to affect the fluorescence signal.

4-Nitrophenylboronic acid (NPBA) was the next absorber studied for fluoride ion sensing. A similar IFE strategy to that employed in the case of MPBA adapts to NPBA as well. The boronic acid head of this molecule is the only reaction center for fluoride ion and Lewis acid-base reaction would be the only reaction pathway. As noted previously, the resonance overlap of electron withdrawing/donating group on the para- position of the benzene ring has a significant effect on acidic power of aromatic acids. Whereas electron donating substituents reduce the acidic power, electron withdrawing groups enhance it [55]. Similarly, for phenylboronic acids, substitution of an electron withdrawing group such as nitro- on the para position is expected to enhance the acidic power and therefore to increase the fluoride ion affinity. Comparison of the UV spectral change of NPBA and PBA upon fluoride addition reflects that the acidic power of NPBA exceeds that of PBA (Fig. 6 A). Therefore, NPBA has the potential to capture lower concentrations of fluoride in water.

Substitution of nitro group on the phenyl ring triggers the spectral red shift from lower concentrations of fluoride. The UV spectrum of NPBA shows a maximum absorption peak at 270nm. This peak shows a gradual red shift of about 13nm upon incremental addition of fluoride ion. Therefore, the signal overlapping between the excitation wavelength of CDs and fluorinated NPBA enhances. The new peak appearing at 283nm could possibly assign to tetrahedral fluorinated adduct. Fig. S4 presents the 19F NMR spectrum of NPBA (0.03M) in 33% (v/v) methanol–D2O on the addition of 10 equivalent of KF. It shows signals at δ −127.46 [KF and RB(OH)2F], −138.4 [RB(OH)F2] and −148.47 [RBF3] [59].

The maximum fluorescence change was achieved with NPBA concentration adjusted to 0.5mM under buffered conditions (phosphate-acetate 0.05M, pH 4) and 5min incubation time. In this experimental condition, fluoride plays just as a nucleophile. Fig. 6B shows that increasing the concentration of fluoride ion to a CDs/NPBA solution gradually enhanced signal suppression. The fluorescence ratio demonstrating a linear range of 0–10mM and LOD value of 1.65×10−4 (3.1ppm) still fulfilling the EPA requirement for water safety.

The selectivity experiments showed that the boron center of NPBA is highly selective for fluoride and has no appreciable affinity for chloride, bromide, acetate, phosphate, and nitrate. While addition of 2.5mM KF results in 20% fluorescence decrease, addition of other anions only results in a change of less than 0.2% (Fig. 6C).Since small fluoride ion is the strongest nucleophile, it is not surprising that other ions do not participate in Lewis acid-base reaction of NPBA. With comparable sensitivity to that of MPBA, in terms of selectivity, this probe could be superior. Moreover, since the changes in the absorbance of the absorber translate into exponential changes in fluorescence of the fluorophore, an enhanced sensitivity and decreased detection limits for the analytical method is reasonable with respect to the absorbance values alone. For example, 10mM fluoride induces 51% fluorescence change while the same concentration only decreases the absorbance to the 28% of the initial value.

N,N-Dimethyl-4-aminophenylboronic acid (DMAPB) as the last arylboronic acid, showed few nanometers of blue shift only in the high concentrations of fluoride. (Fig. S5). Furthermore, this molecule has a dual fluorescence emission at 360 and 462nm originating from locally excited (LE) and TICT states, respectively [60]. The fluorescence mechanism of DMAPB through the two possible pathways has been illustrated in Scheme 2 . Since this molecule shows sharp fluorescence intensity, the necessity of using CDs as the signal transduction units was rectified and the fluorescence emission of this molecule alone was monitored. This made the fluoride sensing system even simpler. Although it has been reported that DMAPB is responsive toward sacharides [60], there is no report of fluoride sensing using this molecule. The boronic acid and dimethyl amine groups on DMAPB as an electron accepting and donating groups play an important role in forming TICT state. This results in a strong fluorescence emission [61].

The hybridization conformational change associated with binding of fluoride to trigonalboronic acid perturbs TICT and induces spectral changes in this molecule. This could directly make the TICT fluorescence emission to be intensively decreased. The boronic acid head which is responsive toward fluoride anion combined with environmentally sensitive dual fluorescence make this molecule a good indicator for fluoride sensing. The pka of the amine functional group has been reported to be 4.33 [60] which means that DMAPB is in its cationic form in acidic conditions. It has been reported that cationic functionality adjacent to the boron atom provides a Columbic drive for anion coordination [31]. Destabilization of the TICT state either by protonation of the amine group or chemical transformation of boronic acid moiety make the fluorescence intensity of DMAPB to be quenched.

Fig. 7 A shows excitation and emission spectra of DMAPB. The excitation peak at 270nm assigns to the TICT fluorescence emission at 460nm. To provide a narrow and sharply peaked profile and to simplify the spectrum, a synchronous scan of DMAPB with Δλ value of 190 was carried out. The fluorescence signal of DMAPB alone was found to be affected upon complexation with fluoride (Fig. 7B). In fact, transformations of trigonalboronic acid to anionic tetrahedral adduct removes TICT and decreases the fluorescence intensity over the concentration range of 2.5–12mM. The fluorescence response of DMAPB toward fluoride ion confirms an efficient fluoride sensing with respect to possible interfering anions (Fig. 7C).

Lakowicz et al. [62] have reported some fluorescent probes based on chalcone and stilbene derivatives for fluoride sensing. Compared to those probes which similarly combine boronic acid and amine groups for TICT process, DMAPB in this work presents two major advantages. First, the large conjugated lengths of previously reported molecules make the use of organic solvents ineviTable Second, those probes show effective fluoride sensing in high concentration ranges (100–200mM). The minimum detectable fluoride concentration using DMAPB was 2.5 Mm while for those compounds reported in the literature with longer conjugation lengths [62], the minimum detected fluoride concentration was as high as 80mM. The significant difference in the sensitivity of DMAPB towards fluoride ion with respect to previous reports is the cationic charge on amine functional group of DMAPB which favors binding of fluoride ion through electrostatic interactions. Moreover, the binding constant for formation of trifluoroarylboronate of DMAPB (Table 1) is two orders of magnitude larger than the unprotonated compound with the same amine and boronic acid functional groups as donor and acceptor, respectively [62]. This observation is consistent with the fact that the columbic host-guest attractions overcome the high hydration enthalpy of fluoride ion in aqueous solution. This gives rise to higher sensitivity towards fluoride ion compared with unprotonated receptor [63].

First a brief discussion is brought on the electronic properties of absorber molecules. Fig. 8 shows the optimized structures of the experimentally synthesized absorbers together with their pictorial description of the FMOs. It is obvious that the absorbers have two types of B-C bonds with the lengths of 1.58Å in X-Ph-B(OH) 2 and 1.62Å in X-Ph-BF3 structures (X=H, SH, SMe, NO2 and NH(Me)2 +).AsNH(Me)2+. As Fig. 8 shows, in all considered arylboronic acids and trifluoroarylboronates, the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) span over the X groups of absorbers while the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) are mostly delocalized along the whole molecular backbone.

It is of interest to note that consistent with the experimental observation, the calculated vertical excited energies of the absorbers nicely reflect the trend of wavelength shifts (Table 2 ). Accordingly, the experimental red shifts of the lowest energy absorption band in structures S−-Ph-B(OH)2 and NO2-Ph-BF3 (33 and 13nm) are well reproduced by the absorption peaks in TDDFT calculations (32 and 12nm) (Table 2). Moreover, the calculated blue shifts for the X-Ph-BF3 (X=SH (18nm), SMe (11nm) and H (13nm)) are in good agreement with the experimental observations (17, 14 and 10nm, respectively). However, such consistency between these theoretical results and experimental band positions confirms the reliability of computational level that was used for this study.

In this work, a combined experimental and theoretical study on water soluble fluoride probes is presented. CDs as the fluorophore and arylboronic acids as the absorbers used for fluoride recognition in aqueous solution. Owing to the small organic backbone of arylboronic acids, use of organic solvents or biphasic conditions was avoided which fulfills the environmental concerns. The simple idea behind these fluorescent assays based on IFE leads to sensitive and selective platform for recognition of fluoride ion in aqueous media. A DFT calculation has also been performed to gain better insight into the role of the absorber, particularly of its electronic structure, on the fluorescence of CD. The experimental red and blue shifts in wavelength observed for absorbers are well reproduced by our theoretical calculations. These calculations clearly show that the structural features which should be incorporated in the receptor structure for enhancing the system sensitivity should (a) be electron withdrawing substituents for increasing the acidic power of the receptor and (b) show large spectral red shift upon reaction with fluoride ion. Based on the experimental results in the present work, incorporation of electron withdrawing groups on the phenyl ring of NPBA could possibly increase the Lewis acidic property and help to construct even more sensitive fluoride probes. In fact, depending on the electronic nature of substituent, the reactivity of these receptors in aqueous media could be simply tuned. As a result, probe sensitivity toward fluoride ion could be manipulated.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of this work by Iran National Elite Foundation and Shiraz University Research Council is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Derivation of the equation used to calculate the binding constants accompanied by an example on PBA receptor; Optimization of the experimental parameters for MPBA response to fluoride ion; Control experiment on MTPBA in response to fluoride ion; UVspectral profile of DMAPB upon addition of fluoride ion; Contributions of calculated vertical excited energy. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jfluchem.2016.08.007.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: USEPA National Drinking Water Regulations EPA 816-F-09-004, May 2009.
